title: TwoDisjointNode

tex_statement.html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W_1, W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of the output nodes of $G$. Then the CDMG obtained from first node-splitting $W_1$ and then node-splitting $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting $W_2$ and then node-splitting $W_1$: \[ \lp G_{\spl(W_1)} \rp_{\spl(W_2)} = \lp G_{\spl(W_2)} \rp_{\spl(W_1)} = G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_7 -- "TwoDisjointNode".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_7 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: TwoDisjointNode
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_7}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_7}
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[Two disjoint node-splittings commute]
   Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W_1, W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of the output nodes of $G$.
   Then the CDMG obtained from first node-splitting $W_1$ and then node-splitting $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting $W_2$ and then node-splitting $W_1$:
   \[ \lp G_{\spl(W_1)} \rp_{\spl(W_2)} =  \lp G_{\spl(W_2)} \rp_{\spl(W_1)}  =  G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}.   \]
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% TODO: write the proof body.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma ‚ÄĒ Two disjoint node-splittings commute

Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W_1, W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of the output nodes of $G$. Then the CDMG obtained from first node-splitting $W_1$ and then node-splitting $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting $W_2$ and then node-splitting $W_1$: \[ \lp G_{\spl(W_1)} \rp_{\spl(W_2)} = \lp G_{\spl(W_2)} \rp_{\spl(W_1)} = G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]

Proof.

lean.name: CDMGEquiv
lean.code:
/-- An equivalence between CDMGs `G : CDMG őĪ` and `H : CDMG ő≤` over
potentially distinct carrier types: a bijection `toEquiv : őĪ ‚ČÉ ő≤` whose
images on the four data fields `J`, `V`, `E`, `L` of `G` recover the
corresponding fields of `H`. The six prop fields (`disjoint_JV`,
`E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL`) are
proof-irrelevant once the four data fields are pinned down. -/
structure CDMGEquiv (G : CDMG őĪ) (H : CDMG ő≤) where
  /-- The underlying bijection on the carrier types. -/
  toEquiv : őĪ ‚ČÉ ő≤
  /-- The bijection sends `G.J` to `H.J`. -/
  J_eq : H.J = toEquiv '' G.J
  /-- The bijection sends `G.V` to `H.V`. -/
  V_eq : H.V = toEquiv '' G.V
  /-- The bijection sends `G.E` to `H.E` (applied to each endpoint). -/
  E_eq : H.E = (Prod.map toEquiv toEquiv) '' G.E
  /-- The bijection sends `G.L` to `H.L` (applied to each endpoint). -/
  L_eq : H.L = (Prod.map toEquiv toEquiv) '' G.L

lean.name: subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V
lean.code:
-- Helper required to state the row (discharges the inner precondition of the iterated splitting in the fusion lemma's type).
/-- If `W‚āā ‚äÜ G.V`, then `Sum.inl '' W‚āā` is contained in the vertex set of
the node-split graph `G.nodeSplittingOn W‚āĀ hW‚āĀ`. Used to discharge the
inner precondition of the iterated splitting in the fusion lemma below. -/
theorem subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V
    {G : CDMG őĪ} {W‚āĀ W‚āā : Set őĪ} (hW‚āā : W‚āā ‚äÜ G.V) (hW‚āĀ : W‚āĀ ‚äÜ G.V) :
    Sum.inl '' W‚āā ‚äÜ (G.nodeSplittingOn W‚āĀ hW‚āĀ).V := by

lean.name: fusionEquiv
lean.code:
/-- The canonical re-labeling bijection
`őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā) ‚ČÉ (őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ) ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(Sum.inl '' W‚āā)` between the
merged-splitting and iterated-splitting carriers. Built by composing
the Mathlib equivalences `Equiv.Set.union` (disjoint-union of sets),
`Equiv.sumAssoc` (sum reassociation), and `Equiv.Set.image` (transport
along an injective embedding). The disjointness hypothesis
`hdisj : Disjoint W‚āĀ W‚āā` is consumed by `Equiv.Set.union`; injectivity
of `Sum.inl` is consumed by `Equiv.Set.image`. -/
noncomputable def fusionEquiv (W‚āĀ W‚āā : Set őĪ) (hdisj : Disjoint W‚āĀ W‚āā) :
    őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā) ‚ČÉ
      (őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ) ‚äē ‚ÜĎ((Sum.inl : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ) '' W‚āā) :=
  letI : DecidablePred (¬∑ ‚ąą W‚āĀ) := Classical.decPred _
  (Equiv.sumCongr (Equiv.refl őĪ) (Equiv.Set.union hdisj)).trans <|
    (Equiv.sumAssoc őĪ ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ ‚ÜĎW‚āā).symm.trans <|
      Equiv.sumCongr (Equiv.refl (őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ))
        (Equiv.Set.image (Sum.inl : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ) W‚āā Sum.inl_injective)

lean.name: nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_7 part 1/2 (fusion lemma): iterated node-splitting is
`CDMGEquiv`-equivalent to a single node-splitting on the union. Mirrors
the first half (`(G_{spl(W‚āĀ)})_{spl(W‚āā)} = G_{spl(W‚āĀ ‚äĒ W‚āā)}`) of the
chained equality in the `\Lem` at
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` line 462. -/
noncomputable def nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv
    {G : CDMG őĪ} {W‚āĀ W‚āā : Set őĪ}
    (hW‚āĀ : W‚āĀ ‚äÜ G.V) (hW‚āā : W‚āā ‚äÜ G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint W‚āĀ W‚āā) :
    CDMGEquiv
      ((G.nodeSplittingOn W‚āĀ hW‚āĀ).nodeSplittingOn
          (Sum.inl '' W‚āā)
          (subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V hW‚āā hW‚āĀ))
      (G.nodeSplittingOn (W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā) (Set.union_subset hW‚āĀ hW‚āā)) := by

lean.name: nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_7 part 2/2 (commute corollary): swapping `W‚āĀ` and `W‚āā` in
the iterated node-splitting yields a `CDMGEquiv`-equivalent CDMG.
Mirrors the second half
(`(G_{spl(W‚āĀ)})_{spl(W‚āā)} = (G_{spl(W‚āā)})_{spl(W‚āĀ)}`) of the chained
equality in the `\Lem` at `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`
line 462. -/
noncomputable def nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv
    {G : CDMG őĪ} {W‚āĀ W‚āā : Set őĪ}
    (hW‚āĀ : W‚āĀ ‚äÜ G.V) (hW‚āā : W‚āā ‚äÜ G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint W‚āĀ W‚āā) :
    CDMGEquiv
      ((G.nodeSplittingOn W‚āĀ hW‚āĀ).nodeSplittingOn
          (Sum.inl '' W‚āā)
          (subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V hW‚āā hW‚āĀ))
      ((G.nodeSplittingOn W‚āā hW‚āā).nodeSplittingOn
          (Sum.inl '' W‚āĀ)
          (subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V hW‚āĀ hW‚āā)) := by

lean_explanation:
The LN states a chained equality $(G_{\spl(W_1)})_{\spl(W_2)} = (G_{\spl(W_2)})_{\spl(W_1)} = G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ for any CDMG $G = (J, V, E, L)$ and any two disjoint subsets $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$. The Lean encoding splits this single LN equation into two `CDMGEquiv`-valued declarations ‚ÄĒ one per `=` in the chain ‚ÄĒ backed by a named canonical bijection `fusionEquiv`.

The ambient vertex type `őĪ : Type u` matches the LN's silent assumption that the graph lives over some carrier; no finiteness instance is needed. `G : CDMG őĪ` is the CDMG; `W‚āĀ W‚āā : Set őĪ` are the two subsets, with `hW‚āĀ : W‚āĀ ‚äÜ G.V` and `hW‚āā : W‚āā ‚äÜ G.V` discharging the LN's $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$ precondition. `Disjoint W‚āĀ W‚āā` is Mathlib's order-theoretic disjointness predicate ‚ÄĒ on sets it amounts to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, matching the LN's `\dcup`.

The node-splitting construction `G.nodeSplittingOn W h` (from def 3.11) produces a CDMG over carrier `őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW`: the disjoint sum of `őĪ` (carrying the LN's $0$-copies $v^0$) with the coerced subtype `‚ÜĎW = {x // x ‚ąą W}` (carrying the $1$-copies $v^1$). Iterating it once for `W‚āĀ` and again for `W‚āā` yields carrier `(őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ) ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(Sum.inl '' W‚āā)`; merging the two as a single splitting on `W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā` yields carrier `őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā)`. These two carriers are not definitionally equal ‚ÄĒ precisely why the row encodes the LN's `=` as `CDMGEquiv` rather than literal `Eq`.

`CDMGEquiv G H` is a five-field bundle: a bijection `toEquiv : őĪ ‚ČÉ ő≤` on carriers plus four image-equalities `J_eq / V_eq / E_eq / L_eq` capturing that `toEquiv` carries the four data fields of `G` to those of `H` (via Mathlib's `Set.image`, written `'' `, and lifted to pairs by `Prod.map` for the edge fields). `fusionEquiv W‚āĀ W‚āā hdisj` is the canonical bijection `őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā) ‚ČÉ (őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ) ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(Sum.inl '' W‚āā)`, composed from `Equiv.Set.union` (disjoint union of sets, consuming `hdisj`), `Equiv.sumAssoc` (associativity of `‚äē`), and `Equiv.Set.image` (transport along the injective embedding `Sum.inl`).

`nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv` is the fusion direction: it takes `hW‚āĀ`, `hW‚āā`, `hdisj` and produces a `CDMGEquiv` between the iterated splitting `(G.nodeSplittingOn W‚āĀ _).nodeSplittingOn (Sum.inl '' W‚āā) _` and the merged splitting `G.nodeSplittingOn (W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā) _`. `nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv` is the commute direction: same hypotheses, `CDMGEquiv` between the two iteration orders. In both, `G`, `W‚āĀ`, `W‚āā` are implicit (recovered from the subset hypotheses); the three subset/disjointness hypotheses are explicit because each is part of the LN's stated precondition.
design_choices:
**`CDMGEquiv` rather than literal `Eq` through a pushforward.** The LN reads its chained equality as set-equality after a re-labeling, but the two CDMGs in question live over *different* carrier types ‚ÄĒ `(őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW‚āĀ) ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(Sum.inl '' W‚āā)` for the iterated splitting versus `őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎ(W‚āĀ ‚ą™ W‚āā)` for the merged one. Literal `Eq` between them is not even type-correct. `CDMGEquiv` is the direction-symmetric encoding: a bijection on carriers plus four image-equalities on `J / V / E / L`. The runner-up was a `CDMG.relabel : CDMG őĪ ‚Üí (őĪ ‚ČÉ ő≤) ‚Üí CDMG ő≤` definition, with the row's conclusion stated as `H = G.relabel e`. That shape is more `rw`-friendly but bakes one direction of the bijection into the result and commits `relabel` chapter-wide before there is evidence of what later carrier-shift consumers (SWIG, counterfactual, iSCM) actually want. The conservative encoding is the structure; promoting to a pushforward later ‚ÄĒ by defining `relabel` once a call-site pattern is visible ‚ÄĒ is cheaper than the reverse move.

**Four bundled image-equalities, no separate component lemmas and no existential.** Spelling the equivalence out via four top-level lemmas `_J_eq`, `_V_eq`, `_E_eq`, `_L_eq` is unfaithful to the LN, which states *one* displayed equation; downstream consumers want "the two CDMGs are equivalent", not four disconnected component facts they restate per direction of the commute. The opposite extreme ‚ÄĒ an `‚ąÉ e, ...` existential ‚ÄĒ hides the canonical witness `fusionEquiv` that the commute corollary needs to name when composing two equivalences. The six prop fields of `CDMG` (`disjoint_JV`, `E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL`) are omitted from `CDMGEquiv`: once the four data-field image-equalities are pinned down, those prop-field obligations on the target CDMG are determined by the source's prop fields plus the bijection. Carrying them inside the bundle adds no information ‚ÄĒ only opportunity for spurious unification mismatches.

**Fusion and commute as separate declarations, mirroring the LN's own proof structure.** Forr√©‚ÄďMooij display their chained equality $(G_{\spl(W_1)})_{\spl(W_2)} = (G_{\spl(W_2)})_{\spl(W_1)} = G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ as one equation but factor their proof in the same shape: verify the fusion direction component-wise, then derive the swap "by symmetry". The Lean split therefore mirrors the LN's split. Downstream consumers that want to *collapse* a chained node-splitting reach for `nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv`; consumers that want to *reorder* two splittings without collapsing them reach for `nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv`. A single chained-equality theorem would force one or the other pattern to project, an extra step every time. Three short groupoid laws (`CDMGEquiv.refl`, `.symm`, `.trans`) are shipped alongside the structure so that the commute corollary is a one-line composition of two fusion-lemma invocations after flipping one via `.symm`; a full `CategoryTheory.Groupoid` instance was rejected because it would force `CDMGEquiv` into a `Sigma`-bundled morphism type over the proper class of all CDMGs across all carrier types, with disproportionate universe and size-issue overhead for a one-row corollary.

**`fusionEquiv` built from Mathlib combinators, marked `noncomputable`.** Rolling the four constructor cases of the bijection (and the two round-trip proofs `left_inv` / `right_inv`) by hand would be roughly thirty lines of `dite`-laden case analysis on `Sum.inl a` versus `Sum.inr w` with `w ‚ąą W‚āĀ` versus `w ‚ąą W‚āā`. The Mathlib triple `Equiv.Set.union` + `Equiv.sumAssoc` + `Equiv.Set.image` packages the same content with the round-trips already discharged, and exposes `_apply`-shaped `simp` lemmas that let the fusion proof reduce the four image-equality goals via `simp` rather than by unfolding hand-rolled match arms. `Equiv.Set.union` requires `DecidablePred (¬∑ ‚ąą W‚āĀ)` to dispatch each $w \in W_1 \cup W_2$ to its $W_1$-or-$W_2$ summand; the instance is supplied via `Classical.decPred`, and the result inherits `noncomputable`. Downstream uses of the fusion lemma are all `Prop`-relevant (in the type of `CDMGEquiv`-valued witnesses), so the classical choice has no observable cost. The codomain wraps in `‚ÜĎ(Sum.inl '' W‚āā)` rather than the cleaner `‚ÜĎW‚āā` because the iterated splitting's second `nodeSplittingOn` call takes that exact `Set` as its argument; avoiding the wrapping would require factoring `nodeSplittingOn` itself to expose a separate carrier-lift helper, which is out of scope for this row.
